data.title: Materialise: Medical Segment Continues To Thrive
data.description:
While Materialise's (NASDAQ:MTLS) Q1 results were a mixed bag, they were well received by investors due to extremely low expectations. The Medical segment continues to grow at a healthy pace, and Materialise's guidance suggests that tariffs will be less of a headwind than initially feared. If Materialise can gain exposure to the defense sector, there could also be tailwinds in Europe.

I previously suggested that manufacturing headwinds were a distraction. Materialise's value is more dependent on its Medical segment, which continues to grow at a healthy pace, and its Software segment, which is steadily transitioning to a subscription model, despite recent revenue weakness. I continue to believe that Materialise will need to stabilize its Manufacturing and Software business and begin improving its margins before the stock moves meaningfully higher though.

Market Conditions

While Materialise has faced a challenging demand environment for some time, increased uncertainty appears to be placing further pressure on its core markets at the moment. In addition to structural issues facing European automotive manufacturers, the imposition of tariffs in the US could impact the global goods economy. Materialise's direct exposure to tariffs isn't that large though, as it has a manufacturing plant in the US that supports its healthcare business and its Software business will not be affected. The company's Manufacturing business is also focused on Europe.

Materialise expects raw material costs to be impacted though, and there is also the likelihood of manufacturing activity deteriorating, leading to less demand for Materialise's services. Manufacturing survey data in the US is currently pointing to this, although the ultimate severity is unclear at this stage.

On a positive note, manufacturing activity in Europe could be poised to rebound somewhat on the back of lower energy costs, increased fiscal stimulus and a desire for greater sovereign defense capabilities. Materialise doesn't appear to have material exposure to the defense sector at this stage though.

Materialise Business Updates

Materialise's Medical segment is being driven by a combination of solid software growth and the adoption of personalized solutions in areas like orthopedics, CMF, cardiac and respiratory. Materialise continues to improve its segmentation, planning and design software to drive further adoption. This includes the introduction of Mimics Flow, an integrated cloud solution which utilizes AI to improve segmentation and planning and enables real-time file sharing. While this software was only introduced in late 2024, customers are reportedly already seeing efficiency gains. Materialise has also introduced segmentation and planning workflows that have been designed for specific applications, like CMF.

In addition, Materialise is focused on expanding its portfolio of medical solutions. The company recently commenced a clinical trial in partnership with the University of Michigan for a 3D-printed, bioresorbable tracheal splint, which is aimed at infants with tracheobronchomalacia. This is a serious condition where the airway can collapse and often requires the use of a ventilator. The splint could help to reduce the burden of the condition until children outgrow it, typically at around the age of three. This will be an 8 year study involving at least 35 infants. If the trial is successful, it could also open the door for other soft tissue applications.

Within its Software segment, Materialise continues to transition to a subscription based business model. The company is also trying to introduce capabilities that help users overcome barriers to broader adoption of additive manufacturing. Materialise continues to iterate on its Magics software, including introducing capabilities that allow users to print more complex parts. The company has also developed two new build processors in partnership with Raplas and One Click Metal. Raplas is an additive manufacturing hardware provider focused on SLA and DLP for production environments. The Raplas build processor has reportedly increased printing speeds by up to 30-40%. One Click Metal offers metal 3D printing systems that are focused on ease of use and affordability. The partnership with One Click Metal is designed to increase Materialise's mid-market exposure.

The performance of Materialise's Software segment has been mixed in recent years. While the transition to recurring revenue is steadily progressing, revenue growth has been weak. Given the current macro environment, I expect printer hardware sales to come under further pressure, undermining Materialise's software revenue.

Materialise's Manufacturing segment also continues to struggle, despite revenue rebounding somewhat in the first quarter. Weakness in Q4 was driven in large part by the European automotive market. With European manufacturers facing market share losses in China and tariffs in the US, this situation could continue to deteriorate. Tariffs could also cause Chinese OEMs to increase their focus on Europe, adding to headwinds.

Materialise is assessing its ability to expand into the defense sector, which could help to offset manufacturing weakness. Given recent political development, Europe appears set to significantly increase its defense investments, with a focus on local capabilities. It will likely take time for Materialise to build a presence in defense though, and it is also not guaranteed that it can gain a foothold in this market. Its success in aerospace is a positive indicator, though.

Financial Analysis

Materialise generated 66.4 million EUR revenue in the first quarter. The Medical segment continues to drive growth, while the Software and Manufacturing segments were impacted by macro uncertainty. Medical software revenue increased 14% YoY, while device and services revenue was up 21%. The decline in Software revenue was also partially driven by an increase in revenue deferral. Macro conditions weighed on Materialise's Manufacturing business. Aerospace was a bright spot though, with sales to that segment increasing 23% YoY. Medtech is another area of strength, but automotive remains a significant headwind.

Materialise continues to expect 270-285 million EUR revenue for the full year, an increase of 4% at the midpoint. This guidance is fairly positive given the recent imposition of tariffs and the fact that foreign exchange is a headwind at the moment. Materialise does expect the second quarter to be more challenging though.

While Materialise's Software revenue continues to be disappointing, deferred revenue related to Software maintenance and license fees is increasing at a solid pace, indicating that Materialise's software business remains healthy. 80% of Materialise's Software revenue is now recurring, up from 75% in the prior quarter.

Materialise's operating expenses increased roughly 7% YoY in Q1, driven by R&D in support of the Medical segment. First quarter EBIT totaled 0.6 million EUR, while cash flow from operations was nearly 10 million EUR. Capital expenditures totaled 1.8 million EUR, declining to a more normal level now that most of the investments related to the new ACTech plant have been completed.

While Materialise expects margins to come under pressure in Q2, I expect margins to begin trending higher going forward. For example, Materialise incurred a number of expenses in 2024 that won't be repeated (integration of FEops, restructuring costs and the ACTech plant start-up). Growth of the Medical segment will also continue to be a structural profitability tailwind and Software margins should recover once the transition to recurring revenue is completed.

Conclusion

The decline of Materialise's share price in recent years is understandable given the post-COVID bubble and the struggles of the business. This has gone too far over the past 2 years though. Materialise is likely to continue generating high single digit revenue growth long-term and should generate healthy double-digit margins once it moves past current headwinds.

Materialise's market capitalization is only around 2.3x its Medical segment revenue, a business that is highly profitable and has grown at a 14% CAGR over the past 5 years. The company also has a net cash position of almost 68 million EUR. This probably doesn't matter while Materialise's margins and revenue remain under pressure from manufacturing weakness. The current situation will not persist indefinitely though, and in the mean time, Materialise's Medical segment will continue to grow in relative importance.

data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals: Q1 Earnings Showcase Growing Momentum
data.description:
As an analyst tracking small-cap biotech names, Rigel Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:RIGL) has always been one of my favorites, thanks to the company’s evolution from a TAVALISSE-centric company to a hematologic biotech that has multiple commercial products. It has been roughly seven months since my last Rigel article, where I reviewed the company’s Q2 2024 earnings and discussed the company’s growth opportunities and how that could have impacted the ticker’s valuation. Since then, the ticker is up over 20% and the company is showing absurd momentum on all fronts, which was showcased in their Q1 earnings, with a strong beat on EPS and revenue.

Rigel looks to be on track to hit or exceed the high end of their $200M–$210M 2025 annual revenue guidance, suggesting the ticker might be ready to transition from a speculative biotech into a more durable, execution-focused growth stock worthy of a spot in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

I intend to review the company’s Q1 earnings highlights, including the revenue acceleration from their three commercial products. In addition, I will go over the company’s profitability metrics, pipeline progress, and IP wins. Then, I will point out a few downside risks that investors should make note of when managing their RIGL position. Finally, I take a look at the charts to see if I can identify an opportunity to manage my RIGL.

Revenue Acceleration

Rigel posted total Q1 revenues of $53.3M, which were up 68% year-over-year. I must say that the level of top-line acceleration is remarkable, but it is important to underline that $43.6M of it came from net product sales, with the rest coming from collaboration and license-related revenues. This is significant because it tells me that Rigel is no longer depending on upfront and milestone payments from partners, but is a durable commercial biotech.

Perhaps the most impressive item to highlight is that TAVALISSE brought home $28.5M in Q1, which was a 35% year-over-year increase. TAVALISSE has been the company’s workhorse for a few years now, so it is encouraging to see both organic demand growth and strong execution at this point in the drug’s commercial lifespan.

Despite the RET inhibitor market being very competitive, GAVRETO has already pulled in $9M in Q1. Meanwhile, REZLIDHIA delivered $6.1M, which is up 25% year-over-year. That level of growth from these drugs points to growing confidence from prescribers and that Rigel is establishing a beachhead in hematology centers.

Rigel’s triad of products is providing them with a commercial base that’s diverse enough to hedge product-specific risk, yet still focused enough to allow them to scale efficiently. One could say that Rigel is operating in a Goldilocks zone that allows them to accelerate their product revenues, without a dramatic increase in expenses, which is ideal for a small-cap commercial-stage biotech.

However, I must also point out that the company still has significant partnership revenue coming. Rigel reported $4.7M from Grifols S.A. (GRFS), with an additional $4.6M coming from Kissei Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:KSPHF ) (TYO: 4547). In addition, Medison Pharma sent Rigel $400K for product deliveries and earned royalties. So, although Rigel is not solely relying on partnership and contract revenue, pulling in nearly $10M in Q1 can be a nice sweetener from time to time.

Proof is in the Profitability

Not only did Rigel have impressive revenue marks, but the company also revealed that their Q1 net income was $11.4M, compared to an $8.2M loss in Q1 of last year. What is more, Rigel’s gross margins improved, while expenses were fairly consistent year-over-year; there was a slight uptick in R&D costs to support their pipeline efforts.

To me, this shows that they are demonstrating operating leverage, where revenue growth is outpacing the costs. Not only does this signal a maturation out of the speculative label, but also gives the company some additional freedom to invest more in their R&D, pipeline, or possibly look for another acquisition… without the need for dilutive fundraising.

Pipeline Progress

Taking a look at Rigel’s pipeline, the company’s lead pipeline program is R289, an IRAK1/4 inhibitor being pitted against relapsed/refractory lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS).

Rigel has completed the dose escalation portion of R289’s Phase Ib study and initiated dosing in the expansion phase. The company also communicated with the FDA about a potential registrational path and plans to present their dose escalation data at a conference in 2H of this year. This program has already secured both Orphan Drug and Fast Track status from the FDA, so the regulatory path is shortened and accelerated.

If the data holds up, R289 might be a major player in the MDS arena and could be entering the market with a first-in-class or best-in-class due to its oral formulation. Roughly 12.2K lower-risk MDS patients have been previously treated, so there’s a significant commercial opportunity that could attract suitors looking for a licensing or partnership with Rigel.

Clarity on TAVALISSE

One important item that didn’t appear to make any big headlines, was the TAVALISSE patent settlement with Annora Pharma. The settlement push a potential generic entry to no earlier than Q2 2032, so Rigel currently has a seven-year runway.

Not only does that ruling allow Rigel to maintain its position in the market, but it also gives the visibility needed to predict cash flows and reduce the number of uncertainties. Not only does this increase the value of TAVALISSE, but extending the lifecycle of their flagship product could give Rigel enough time to start developing another drug or acquire a replacement product.

Remnant Risks

Although I just went over some bullish highlights from the company’s Q1 earnings, I must point out that risks remain for Rigel. Despite the recent commercial success of their three product, the company is still heavily dependent on TAVALISSE, GAVRETO, and REZLIDHIA revenue to stay afloat. As a result, a disruption in one of those products could generate an abrupt change in the company’s outlook. Keep in mind, the competition in both the RET and IDH1-mutant AML markets is incredibly intense, so investors shouldn’t just chalk up growth for GAVRETO and REZLIDHIA for the next five years.

Investors should also keep an eye on R&D spending as it continues to climb in order to maintain their clinical efforts. Although R289 appears to be a promising drug, investment in the program will be costly as we move deeper into development. Therefore, Rigel must maintain their profitability to avoid being forced to perform dilutive financing to maintain their pipeline programs, commercial efforts, and keep the lights on. Reminder, while the Annora settlement removes near-term patent risk on TAVALISSE until 2032, we can also say there is now a countdown for Rigel to either find a replacement or amass enough new revenue streams before the loss of exclusivity of their flagship product.

Essentially, all the positive items I highlighted in this earnings review still have remnant risk. Although the company has an ample amount of momentum, investors need to see that it is a delicate balance that needs to be maintained if Rigel is to become a reliable growth stock. I must point out that the company only has ~$77.1M in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, so the company cannot afford for more than one thing to fall out of place before legitimate fears of dilution begin to rise.

Despite these risks, I am maintaining my RIGL conviction level at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio.

Looking for a Buy

The market’s initial reaction to Rigel’s Q1 earnings was very positive. However, the rally was short-lived, as the share price found resistance right on a trendline at $25.42 per share, the day after the earnings report, and the ticker sold off going into the close. Now, the ticker is trading at $18.25 per share, which is below my current RIGL Buy Threshold of $20.45.

As a result, I am searching for a potential buy if the ticker can break and hold above that $17.30 resistance level. Subsequently, I will look to make another addition if the ticker can hold above the 200-Day EMA around $17.75 per share. Admittedly, I wouldn’t be making a huge addition because the technicals aren’t incredibly bullish, but I am comfortable adding based on the company’s growth and momentum.

Once I have added to the position, I will add on to some of my sell orders around my Sell Targets at $40.10 and $49 per share in order to book profit and move the RIGL position back to a “House Money” position. Ultimately, my plan is to repeat this process a least one more time in order to generate profits while growing a core position to transfer to my RIGL position to my “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

data.title: Logan Energy: Building At A Brisk Pace
data.description:
The last article on Logan Energy (OTCPK:LOECF) noted how experienced management can build a company rapidly. The specifics at the time included the latest acquisition made. There is another big year ahead for rapid growth scheduled for fiscal year 2025. This management notes (yet again) in the latest corporate presentation that they are builders. But they manage to rapidly build in such a way that investors benefit.

Earnings

Even though the earnings report has a lot of startup and nonrecurring costs for the company's first full year of existence, both cash flow per share and earnings per share improved.

(Note: This is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Keep in mind that the company came into being as a spinoff in fiscal year 2023 (roughly about midyear). The management experience showed in that the company took advantage of several opportunities that came by while still growing cash flow and earnings despite the significant increase in shares outstanding.

This happened despite the fact that the price of natural gas averaged significantly lower than was the case in fiscal year 2023. With production as low as it was here, this company is a price taker with little to no bargaining power.

Those who took advantage of the financing to exercise the warrants at C$0.30 per share have a nice profit in the form of stock price appreciation. That does not often happen in the first full fiscal year either.

Guidance

The company has another big year planned in the drive to get to a production level that will minimize costs at a reasonable level.

Notice the line about nine wells coming online with the startup of a gas processing plant that is now under construction. That happens late in the fiscal year. Yet the company is guiding to a significant jump in fiscal year average production. That should mean that the exit rate will be substantially higher than the average production.

Therefore, investors can assume that fiscal year 2026 will at least begin with production much higher than the average for fiscal year 2025. Fiscal year 2026 will again show a rapid growth rate.

But the rapid growth can be handled with management experience. This allows unit costs to come in-line with acceptable levels throughout the industry for the basin where the properties are located. That also means that earnings and cash flow per share should take another sizable jump in the current fiscal year.

Pouce Coupe Growth

The first step in the 2025 budget is to grow the cash flow source to help fund future growth ideas.

One of the first cash raises in the past fiscal years was to fund the construction of the gas processing plant and oil battery. This happened the minute that management was able to obtain more natural gas transportation capacity.

Notice that this acreage also has the Charlie Lake formation that so many neighboring competitors have found to be very profitable. Even though plans call for this acreage to produce 10,000 BOED, there is a possibility of that plan enlarging in the future.

In the meantime, the exit production rate for this acreage should more than double in the current fiscal year.

The big deal here is shown on the slide above. This was long known as a natural gas producing area for the reason shown above. The well simply did not go deep enough. Now that the wells can profitably get to the interval shown, the liquids production makes the acreage far more profitable than it was in the past.

This story is repeated over much of the company acreage.

Simoette

This is the other acreage position with significant production already and growth in that production for fiscal year 2025.

The opportunities on this acreage have steadily expanded during the past fiscal year as more intervals will possibly prove to be commercial. The liquids percentage of production is likely to increase as wells are drilled to intervals that have a higher percentage of the production mix that is liquids.

This is yet another part of Canada that was known for its natural gas production. But technology has advanced to the point where the liquids can be obtained as part of the production. It has dramatically increased the profitability of the area.

Summary

The company has the growth prospects shown and more. Now the pace of growth will slow once the initial startup steps complete. This management clearly wants to get to a level of production that allows for optimization of operating costs.

This company has clearly chosen to raise capital along the way to both grow quickly to a minimum size while also keeping debt ratios conservative. There are others out there that may choose to grow slower or may choose to use more debt. Personally, I believe that the risk of failure is lower if the costs quickly are optimized by getting to that minimum optimal level. I would therefore be in favor of the pathway that the company chose.

Trying to compare quarterly reports is a nightmare in a situation like this because the previous fiscal year is likely not comparable due to materially different conditions. Investors need to rely upon the experience of management and the quarterly goals given (to the extent they are given) at this stage of the company development.

After this year, visibility and quarterly comparisons will likely start to become meaningful. The strong buy with a speculative risk level remains. Any new company startup is a high risk. But management experience lowers that risk, as does the choice of a conservative financial position.

Risk

The political uncertainty with regard to Canada and the United States can be particularly critical for a company like this. I covered many companies whose transition to an operating model was interrupted by fiscal year 2020 challenges. Many of those companies have struggled since then. This article is written with the idea that something will be negotiated that is both permanent and constructive so that the uncertainty declines materially.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn could materially alter future plans or the company outlook. Management has already noted the flexibility of its plans, while also noting that they are closely watching the political situation. That makes the low debt strategy almost mandatory given the uncertainty the company is now facing.

A loss of key personnel could materially damage the future prospects of the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Herbalife: In A Better Place, But Not Out Of The Woods
data.description:
Not Yet Pulling Up Trees, But Doing Alright

In the first month of 2025, we had written about the global nutrition company Herbalife (NYSE:HLF) when it wasn’t having its finest hour. While HLF has since experienced a relatively volatile ride, it has still managed to perform in line with other consumer staples stocks, and also significantly outperform its peers from the Russell 2000 that are down by -14% on average.

Now, given that around three-and-a-half months have passed, and we’ve also seen the release of two earnings events (the latest of which, only took place last week), we felt an update was due on some of the key subplots associated with HLF.

Third Time Lucky?

One of the most important developments of late, has been the ascent of Stephan Gratziani who has taken over the role of CEO since the turn of this month. Meanwhile the previous CEO, Michael Johnson (who also served as Chairman of the board), has moved on to the post of Executive Chairman. As noted previously, Gratziani has, in the past, been associated with Herbalife as an independent distributor (for over three decades), but then eventually moved in-house and took up the role of Chief Strategy Officer in 2023.

It is rather evident that the HLF board views Gratziani with great regard, as they had tried to sign him up in 2011, and now they’ve promoted him to the top post in less than two years. However, those who’ve been following HLF over the years would note that top management transition hasn’t always had the desired effect. In fact, there have been two separate instances over the last six years, where Michael Johnson has had to return and take on the mantle of CEO because his successors weren’t able to execute effectively enough. Will Johnson be third time lucky?

Distributors Are Coming On Board, But The Runrate Has Dipped

As noted earlier, Gratziani was brought on board to help end the decline of new distributors (Herbalife members who resell products to consumers or those who recruit and run a sales team that sells these products) that had been on a downward trajectory for 12 straight quarters Q2-24. He certainly appears to be delivering on this front, as new distributors for Herbalife have been growing for four straight quarters now, although the pace of growth did dip in Q1 to 16% (after three consecutive quarters of improving YoY growth).

Going forward, over the next couple of quarters we suspect Herbalife may find it tough to grow its distributors at such heightened double-digit levels, as the positive comp effect kicks off from Q2-25 (until Q2-24, distributor growth was negative, and it was thus easier to put up elevated YoY numbers).

However, by Q4, and more crucially in FY26, we expect another fillip as the company will roll out the Pro2col technology platform in full form (although the beta version will be released in the US in Q3 itself). Pro2col, which leverages individual data and biometric to provide a more personalized health and nutrition regime for consumers, should empower distributors to compete better in the market-place, and attract even more distributors. Interestingly enough, Pro2Col’s founder- Blake Mallen will now take over Gratziani’s old post of Chief Strategy Officer, so it's perhaps reasonable to expect HLF to transition more to a tech-infused distributor strategy going forward.

Underlying Business Is Growing, With Potentially Minimal Impact From Tariffs

Passive observers of Herbalife may be wondering what good is all this distributor growth, if the topline isn’t necessarily growing; well that would be the takeaway if you’re only looking at reported revenue growth, which continues to be on a downward slump for yet another quarter (most recently it declined by 3.4% YoY in Q1). However, this does not reflect the underlying trends within the business and is heavily skewed by currency translation which ended up damaging HLF’s Q1 topline by over $60M.

If you discount that, note that underlying growth has been positive in three out of the past four quarters, and grew at 1.4% more recently.

Besides, the outlook for the rest of the year looks even better with management guiding to 3% (mid-point of guidance) FX adjusted growth for the FY.

For those wondering about tariffs, Herbalife has clarified that it is “not material” to their FY results as half their ingredient imports from China get re-exported out, resulting in a duty drawback of 50%. To reiterate, at $10-$15m on an annualized basis, it is only around one-fifth the adverse impact that FX translation left on the Q1 topline.

Going forward, note that revenue growth trends actually look a lot healthier with consensus now budgeting for mid-single-digit revenue growth (on average) in FY26 and FY27.

Further Deleveraging Progress, Despite Weak Cash Generation

Q1 wasn’t a very robust cash flow quarter for HLF, with the operating cash flow dropping to its lowest point in five years (less than half a million), and also not meaningful enough to cover the CAPEX bill, resulting in negative cash flow.

However, investors should not get overly bogged down, as the Q1 cashflow position usually gets adversely affected by annual distributor bonuses that are paid out in March, as well as employee performance bonuses, all of which amount to hundreds of millions of dollars in aggregate.

Besides, HLF deserves credit for continuing to bring down its CAPEX commitments per quarter (also CAPEX as a function of sales has more than halved from the levels seen in 2023).

Crucially, despite a bland cash generation quarter, HLF still managed to pay down $70m of total debt, taking its gross leverage down to 3x. HLF is ahead of schedule in this regard because it was previously only expected to hit 3x leverage by the end of FY25.

Having said that, we don’t want to play down the task at hand, as the outstanding principal amount of total debt is still quite hefty at $2260m (Management’s goal is to bring this to $1400M by 2028). However, what’s key to note is that once it pays off its 2025 senior notes equating to $197m this year (before September), it will have some breathing room in 2026 and 2027, before the 2028 convertible comes due.

Compelling Valuations

One of the major incentives to consider HLF is that its valuations remain dirt cheap. To elaborate, amongst the 22 personal care product stocks from Seeking Alpha’s universe, HLF is the second-cheapest stock, at a forward P/E multiple of just 3.4x; this multiple is not even one-fifth of the industry average of over 19x! In addition to that, HLF’s multiple also represents a 37% discount over its own rolling average of 5.4x

The obvious conclusion to draw from such a low P/E multiple is that the cadence of earnings growth will be very minimal, and while that is the base case for the current year (less than 3% YoY growth), over the next couple of years, earnings growth looks quite exceptional at over 30% p.a. (on average).

Then, even though it was a disappointing cash flow quarter (mainly due to seasonality which will reverse in the quarters ahead), on an annualized basis, the FCF yield you could lock in at the current market-cap is still exceptionally sturdy at 23.5%, almost twice as good as the stock’s 5-year average.

Closing Thoughts- Bottom In Place, But Not Able To Kick On

Based on what the charts are suggesting, we feel the neutral stance ought to be maintained as we see both good and bad developments.

Firstly, as opposed to the last time we covered it, when it was still experiencing a series of lower-lows and lower-highs, it looks like HLF has made a bottom for now. Note that after dropping to the $5 levels in February, HLF has not retested those lows, but has managed to form a higher low, in the subsequent bout of selling.

Investors may also want to take note of the waning appetite of short-sellers, with the total shares sold short, declining by 12% over the past three months.

However, we still remain concerned by HLF’s inability to break past the descending trend line resistance that has been serving as resistance for well over two years now. In March, HLF did its best to breakout from here, but couldn’t sustain the bullish momentum, and was then prone to some selling pressure.

Even conditions on the shorter time frame daily chart reiterate the challenges that HLF is facing to build any sort of bullish momentum. We generally don’t encourage a long position when a stock is trading below its 200-day moving average, as uptrends generally don’t have a great deal of ammunition. We’re seeing that with HLF which continually keeps showing fatigue as it approaches its 200DMA, and reflects the suboptimal confidence that the bulls have in the stock.

data.title: Archer Aviation: A Flying Dream Or Grounded Reality?
data.description:
Introduction

Archer Aviation (NYSE:ACHR) is a startup that plans to produce, sell, and lease electric vertical take-off and landing vehicles, known as eVTOLs. Currently, the company is in the process of obtaining certifications from the FAA – Federal Aviation Administration – and has already secured three approvals:

Part 135 – Air Carrier Certificate. This allows Archer to operate as an airline, although initially using only conventional aircraft.Part 141 – Pilot Training Academy. This certification ensures that Archer can train its pilots to operate the Midnight aircraft in commercial air taxi services.Part 145 – Aircraft Repair Services. Grants Archer the ability to carry out necessary maintenance on the aircraft, supporting both air taxi operations and post-sale maintenance services.

The fourth and final approval before commercial launch, Part 142, which regulates training centers and flight simulators, has not yet been approved, but is expected by year-end. However, these are not the only requirements for the company’s official start. Additional certifications needed include:

  • Type Certification, which approves the aircraft's design. This is in progress and expected to be completed in 2025;
  • Production Certification, which authorizes mass production and can only be requested after the Type Certification is approved;
  • Airworthiness Certificate, ensuring the eVTOL complies with approved specifications;
  • Local Authorizations, which permit takeoff and landing at vertiports, heliports, and urban areas. In this regard, Archer has been maintaining relationships with local authorities to meet the requirements.

Once licenses are secured, Archer’s business will be split into two units: Archer UAM, focused on air taxi services, and Archer Direct, focused on selling aircraft to other companies.

Within the UAM unit, Archer aims to compete with existing alternatives like Uber, taxis, and private ground transport, as well as helicopter operators in the air. In this model, trips are expected to be short, around 20 miles, with 4 passengers and one pilot. Flights are expected to last 10–20 minutes (vs. 1 hour by car).

On pricing, Archer’s initial models estimate that the cost per passenger will be below Uber Black fares and will decline as operations scale.

In my opinion, despite the savings from not using fuel, the long-term plan seems extremely optimistic and not currently feasible.

In the sales unit, Archer Direct, the plan is to reach a gross margin between 40% and 50%, which should occur once production hits 250 units per year.

Based on firm orders, the estimated price per aircraft is around USD 5 million. The order backlog totals approximately USD 6 billion, covering 1,141 units.

To support its commercial plan across both segments, Archer estimates total production of 650 units per year by 2028, surpassing a 50% gross margin.

Financial History & Projections

To date, Archer does not yet have a detailed financial history, as it has not been authorized to provide services. To finance its operations, Archer has conducted several capital raises, as its operations cannot yet support the investments needed to obtain approvals.

The increase in expenses has been driven by progress on approvals, which forced the company to expand its team to advance aircraft production. This trend is expected for a pre-operational company.

Currently, Archer has USD 855 million in cash, which, considering the 2024 cash burn, gives it a lifespan of approximately two years, aligned with the company's plan to reach gross profits of USD 500–600 million, as shown in the image above.

If all goes as planned, Archer likely won't need to raise more capital. However, it's feasible that cash burn will increase in the coming years if delays occur.

Thinking ahead, let’s sketch out some projections for potential scenarios, using the company's proposed scenario as the most optimistic:

Pessimistic Scenario:

I assumed sales in 2028 would be 50% lower than in the optimistic case. With lower fixed cost dilution, gross margin drops from 55% to 40%. For SG&A, I estimated a 5% annual adjustment to existing expenses and a sales expense equal to 10% of revenue.

In this case, even by 2028, the company still wouldn’t generate positive cash flow, measured by EBITDA. When you add required capex and interest expenses, the company would not be operationally viable without another capital raise after 2026.

Base Scenario:

I assumed 2028 sales would be 25% below the optimistic scenario. Gross margin drops from 55% to 50%. SG&A same as above.

Here, the company becomes viable, but will need capital until the end of 2027. After that, it will probably generate cash and sustain operations with its own profits.

Optimistic Scenario:

As in the base case, the company transforms from dream to profitable investment, generating cash and sustaining growth. In this case, Archer would indeed be a strong investment opportunity, reaching a low EBITDA multiple (4x), despite rapid growth and a promising market.

Before proceeding, in all scenarios I assume that the company will allocate all production to sales, excluding revenue and costs from air taxi operations. The reason is the high variability of assumptions, which would make estimates unstable.

If part of the production is allocated to air taxi operations, expected revenue would be even lower, since service revenues accrue over time—making the optimistic model even less realistic.

Another concern: pre-orders are being taken now. The company has committed to delivering over 1,000 eVTOLs without having FAA production approval. This timing mismatch between order intake and delivery could severely impact expected margins.

In other words, the company’s future gross margin likely won’t hit 50%, at least not while fulfilling current orders.

Conclusion & Risks

If we only consider the optimistic scenario, which overestimates margins and revenue, Archer would be an excellent investment—trading at 1.2x revenue and 4x EBITDA—especially considering the total addressable market (TAM) is projected to be USD 9 trillion by 2050.

However, there are many “ifs,” which raise the investment's risk level.

Currently, I prefer two other eVTOL players over Archer: Joby (JOBY), which is ahead in approvals and has Toyota as a partner, and Eve (EVEX), which, despite lagging in approvals, is backed by Embraer.

There are two distinct theses:

  • If you believe in the market’s long-term potential, Joby is better positioned.
  • If you have a higher risk appetite and seek higher upside (and higher risk), Eve is more compelling.

A note: despite Eve being more embryonic than Archer, having Embraer as a partner provides a sense of delivery, development, and operational capability.

Another point of concern: Archer’s main competitor, Joby, plans to produce 500 units per year—150 fewer than Archer.

Archer, like many startups, has a strong history of cash burn and lofty promises, which have driven shares higher in recent years.

Indeed, if the company delivers on its promises, it could be a great buy. But will they deliver?

In traditional industries like car manufacturing, companies often fail to meet production targets—Tesla itself has suffered from this “overpromise and underdeliver” issue. Why would a new market be any different?

For now, my recommendation is HOLD.

Reevaluation will occur if:

  • They prove in 2025 that they can deliver – I’ll upgrade to BUY;
  • If cash burn exceeds expectations, certification delays, or plan changes – I’ll downgrade to SELL.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

International equities rose in the first quarter. Financial markets were rattled by seismic shifts in US foreign policy, altering global trade, growth, and inflation outlooks. Enthusiasm for AI- related expansion and a more accommodative US business environment waned due to rising economic and tariff uncertainty. Capital rotated away from the US to international markets, particularly Europe, which outperformed amid economic signs, including less restrictive monetary policy, improving manufacturing output, and greater fiscal stimulus. In Germany, the center-right Christian Democratic Union and the Christian Social Union won the federal election, sparking optimism for economic revitalization. The new coalition government unveiled spending plans to boost economic growth and defense, with total spending likely to exceed €1 trillion. In March, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent outlined an ambitious vision to reduce the US deficit to 3%–3.5% of GDP by 2028 while avoiding a recession—US GDP grew by 2.8% in 2024, and the federal deficit was 6.4% of GDP. The plan aims to shift growth back into the private sector through deregulation and affordable energy. China’s economy expanded by 5% in 2024, meeting the government’s target. Its State Council announced a plan to bolster consumption and the economy, including tax incentives and subsidies for key industries.

The MSCI ACWI ex USA Index returned 5.23% for the period. Within the Index, 10 out of 11 sectors rose over the quarter. Communication services and financials were the top-performing sectors.

Performance Summary

The Hartford International Opportunities Fund (I Share) outperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index during the quarter.

Security selection was a driver of relative performance. Strong selection in financials and communication services was partially offset by selection in industrials. On a regional basis, selection in Developed EU and Middle East ex UK detracted most.

Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock-selection process, detracted from returns. An overweight to information technology was partially offset by our overweight to communication services.

BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY) (industrials) was the top relative and absolute contributor, TFI International (TFII) (industrials) was the top relative detractor, and Taiwan Semiconductor (information technology) was the top absolute detractor during the period.

Positioning & Outlook

International equity markets had a positive return for the first quarter of 2025, as shifts in US policy led investors to seek opportunities elsewhere. As we look toward 2025, we believe volatility may remain elevated as the market continues to be acutely focused on the implications of policy changes from the Trump administration, macroeconomic issues such as the level of rates, as well as geopolitical tensions in certain regions around the world.

We may look to take advantage of the volatility by investing in companies with improving or sustainable returns on capital that we believe are underappreciated by the market.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

These risks may be greater, and include additional risks, for investments in emerging markets or if the Fund focuses in a particular geographic region or country. • Mid-cap securities can have greater risks and volatility than large-cap securities. • The securities of large market capitalization companies may underperform other segments of the market. • To the extent the Fund focuses on one or more sectors, the Fund may be subject to increased volatility and risk of loss if adverse developments occur.
